notes: First-pass curated set. Quotes are either direct (with source URL) or
clearly attributed paraphrases of public essays / podcast appearances.
Themes align with screener/li_engine/themes.yaml where possible.
Tickers are illustrative — used by the matcher to surface a quote on a card.

quotes.quote: Counting the OOMs from GPT-4, another jump of similar size lands us at models that are drop-in remote workers. The straight line on the log plot is the most important fact in the world right now.
We are going to need trillion-dollar compute clusters by the end of the decade. The bottleneck is no longer chips — it is power, land, and the ability to pour concrete fast enough.

American electricity production has been roughly flat for decades. Training a 100 GW cluster will require building out generation at a pace the country has not seen since the 1960s — and natural gas is the only thing that can move that fast.
Superintelligence is a national security issue. The free world will need to out-build the CCP on compute and energy, or we will be racing for second place on the most important technology in history.
Algorithmic secrets and model weights are the crown jewels. If we cannot secure them at state-actor level, the lead the United States holds is fictional.

Once you have automated AI research, the intelligence explosion compresses a decade of algorithmic progress into a year. Compute scaling is the ignition; recursive self-improvement is the chain reaction.
Memory bandwidth, not flops, is increasingly the binding constraint on training. HBM capacity is the silent kingmaker of this cycle.
By the late 2020s, the AI labs that matter will look more like Manhattan-Project-style state efforts than Silicon-Valley startups. Capital intensity alone forces consolidation.

NVIDIA's moat is not just CUDA, it is the entire systems stack — networking, software, reference designs. Competitors are not selling chips against chips; they are selling Lego against a finished building.

Apple's distribution of on-device AI to a billion-plus active devices is a structural advantage no model lab can replicate. The question is whether the silicon roadmap keeps pace with the model roadmap.
DeepSeek did not break the scaling laws — it broke the assumption that the frontier required ever-larger CapEx. Inference, not training, is where the next decade of compute spend lives.
Hyperscaler capex of $200 billion-plus a year is the single largest non-government infrastructure build in modern history. Pick-and-shovel beats picks-of-models.
If reinforcement-learning on verifiable tasks scales the way pre-training did, the labs that own the most compute and the cleanest reward signals win — and that is a small list.
Zuckerberg sized Llama clusters by the gigawatt. When the unit of capacity for AI training is a power plant, the bottleneck has obviously shifted off silicon.
Tokens-per-dollar is the only KPI that matters once the models commoditize. The hyperscaler that drives that curve fastest takes the inference market.
Power is the constraint. By 2030 a single training run will consume more electricity than New York City — and no one is building grid for it yet.
Software-defined vehicles and autonomous robotaxi networks turn cars into data centers on wheels. Tesla's full-stack approach captures the cash-flow shift from hardware margin to platform economics.
Bitcoin is a monetary network, not a tech stock. Spot ETF flows institutionalize it as a portfolio-construction asset alongside gold and treasuries.
Multiomic biology — gene editing, AI-designed proteins, single-cell sequencing — is on the same exponential cost curve that semiconductors rode for fifty years.
Public-blockchain rails will eat traditional payments. Coinbase and the equity-proxy miners are the picks-and-shovels of the on-chain economy.

The U.S. needs to build a hundred gigawatts of new generation just to run the AI we are about to deploy. Independent power producers and electrical-equipment names are the most asymmetric trade of the decade.
Sovereign AI is the next sovereign-wealth play. Every G20 country is going to spend on its own model stack and its own data centers, and almost none of that revenue exists yet.
The next ten thousand unicorns will be one-person companies running on agents. The picks-and-shovels are inference compute and orchestration software.
Accelerated computing has reached a tipping point. General-purpose computing has run its course; the world's data centers are being rebuilt from the ground up around AI.
Sovereign AI infrastructure is no longer optional for nation states. Every country wants its own intelligence, on its own soil, in its own language.
Robotics is the next industrial revolution. The combination of foundation models and physical AI turns every factory into a software-defined facility.
It is possible that in the next few thousand days we will have superintelligence. The bottleneck — and the opportunity — is energy and compute, not algorithms.
The cost of intelligence is going to fall dramatically and the demand for it will rise faster — that is the Jevons paradox of the next decade.
Agents that can do real work over hours and days are the unlock for productivity. Most knowledge work will be augmented or automated within five years.
Fiscal dominance has replaced monetary dominance. Persistent deficits mean every recession will be met with stimulus, and hard assets — energy, gold, bitcoin — outperform across the cycle.
The U.S. natural-gas complex is a structural winner of the AI build-out. LNG export capacity plus baseload data-center demand puts a floor under the entire midstream.
Bitcoin's role as collateral in the global financial system is being institutionalized. Equity proxies that hold large balance-sheet positions are leveraged calls on that thesis.
The U.S. grid is the binding constraint on AI ambition. You cannot 'install more cloud' without first installing more copper, more transformers, and more megawatts.
Nuclear renaissance is no longer aspirational — hyperscaler PPAs have priced it. Operating fleet owners and SMR developers are the cleanest plays on data-center baseload.
Natural gas is the bridge fuel that will actually be built. Combined-cycle turbines deliver gigawatts in 24 months; nuclear and renewables cannot match that schedule.
Critical-minerals supply chains are a strategic vulnerability. Western processing capacity for uranium, copper, and rare earths is years behind demand.
